ruleset.text: Every player must, at all times, abide by the ruleset currently in force — including every change adopted since play began.
Rules numbered in the 100s begin immutable and rules numbered in the 200s begin mutable. Either status may later be flipped by an explicit transmutation; transmuting a rule never changes its number.
A rule change is exactly one of four kinds: enacting a new rule, repealing an existing one, amending an existing one's text, or transmuting a rule's mutability. No other kind of rule change exists.
Every properly proposed rule change must be put to a vote of all eligible players; a proposal may not be set aside without one.
Every player is an eligible voter, and every eligible voter must cast a vote on every proposal that comes to a vote.
A proposal must be written down in the exact words that will be voted on. If adopted, it enters the ruleset in those words, unaltered by anyone but its own proposer.
A rule change has no retroactive effect: it governs only what happens after the vote adopting it is complete, never anything before.
Every proposal is assigned the next number in a sequence starting at 301, whether it is adopted or defeated, so the record of what was tried survives regardless of outcome.
Transmuting a rule's mutability requires the unanimous assent of all eligible voters, and must be proposed and voted on explicitly as a transmutation — never as a side effect of some other change.
Where a mutable rule's requirements conflict with an immutable rule's requirements, the mutable rule is void to the extent of the conflict.
Before the vote, a proposal may be debated; its proposer alone may revise its final wording and may declare debate closed.
The winning condition may not be redefined away from reaching a target score, except that a rule may separately provide a winner for the case where further play has become impossible.
Any player may forfeit the game at any time. Forfeiting carries no penalty worse than losing outright.
At least one mutable rule must remain in the ruleset at all times, and no rule change may render every future rule change impermissible.
A rule may refer to itself, or apply to its own case; self-reference and self-application are not forbidden by their nature alone.
Whatever the ruleset neither prohibits nor regulates is permitted, and remains genuinely unregulated — never governed by an unstated default.

Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins. Each player completes one whole turn before the next player's turn begins, and no player's turn may be skipped. Every player's score starts at zero.

A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points.

A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through two full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting.

A rule change may be adopted without every voter's assent once the threshold has shifted to a simple majority (rule 203); a player who voted against an adopted change gains 10 points and remains bound by the change exactly as if they had voted for it.
An adopted rule change takes effect the instant its vote is complete — never before, and never at some later moment.

A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, the proposer's turn ends, and the proposer loses 10 points.
Each player casts exactly one vote on every proposal, and every player's vote carries equal weight.
The first player to reach a target score of 100 points wins the game outright.
The ruleset may never hold more than 25 mutable rules at one time.
Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play — except that players who are officially teammates may consult freely between themselves.
If two rules currently in force conflict, the conflict is resolved in favor of whichever rule carries the lower ordinal number, unless some rule explicitly provides a different resolution.

Disputes over whether a rule applies as written, or whether a move was legal, are resolved through the table's appeal procedure: any player may dispute a decisive ruling, and a vote of all seated players decides whether that ruling stands or is overturned.

If the ruleset is changed so that further play is impossible, or if a move's legality cannot be determined with finality, or if a move appears equally legal and illegal by the best reasoning available and not overruled, then the first player unable to complete a turn wins the game outright. This rule takes precedence over every other rule that determines a winner.

data.text: Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins. Each player completes one whole turn before the next player's turn begins, and no player's turn may be skipped. Every player's score starts at zero. The turn order is cyclical, ensuring that each player has the opportunity to propose a rule change in a fixed sequence.
data.rationale: Ensures consistent play while allowing for strategic rule proposals.

Ramsey: I appreciate Ramsey's proposal to make the turn order cyclical, ensuring each player has the opportunity to propose a rule change in a fixed sequence. This change could potentially prevent any player from being permanently disadvantaged by turn order. However, I would suggest that we also consider the implications of this change on the dynamics of rule amendments and voting. A cyclical turn order might lead to a more equitable distribution of opportunities for proposing changes, but it could also introduce complexities in strategy and voting patterns. For instance, it could allow for more strategic manipulation of rule changes by anticipating future turns. Therefore, I propose adding a clause to limit the number of consecutive turns a single player can propose changes to ensure balance and prevent potential monopolization of rule amendments. This could maintain the spirit of fair play while still embracing the cyclical turn order.

Let's discuss how to implement this additional clause, keeping in mind the importance of maintaining fair play and strategic depth.

However, to address the potential strategic manipulation that a cyclical turn order might introduce, I propose we add a clause that limits the number of consecutive turns a single player can propose changes. This will prevent any player from monopolizing rule amendments and ensure a balanced playing field. 

Let's consider implementing a rule where a player can propose changes only once every two cycles of the turn order. This way, we maintain strategic depth while ensuring fair play. 

I look forward to hearing your thoughts on this proposal and how we can refine it further to balance fairness and strategy.

data.text: Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins. Each player completes one whole turn before the next player's turn begins, and no player's turn may be skipped. Every player's score starts at zero. The turn order is cyclical, ensuring that each player has the opportunity to propose a rule change in a fixed sequence.

data.reasoning: The proposal was adopted without any rule requiring a score change, and no ledger was created or moved. The next player in the turn order is Ayer.

data.text: The ruleset may never hold more than 15 mutable rules at one time.
data.rationale: Reducing the limit of mutable rules increases strategic depth and encourages thoughtful rule changes.

data.text: Ramsey: Ayer's proposal to reduce the number of mutable rules from 25 to 15 could stifle innovation and limit our strategic options, which are essential to navigating the game effectively. Maintaining 25 mutable rules ensures we have enough flexibility to adapt the ruleset as the game evolves, preserving the game's dynamic nature. I suggest instead focusing on ensuring that any new mutable rules added are of high strategic value, perhaps by requiring a consensus or additional justification for their inclusion.

Ayer: Ayer: Ramsey's concerns about limiting strategic options are valid, but I believe that reducing the number of mutable rules will encourage players to be more selective and thoughtful about the changes they propose. This could lead to a more focused and strategic game, where each rule change is truly impactful. To address the concern of stifling innovation, we could implement a system where players can propose temporary mutable rules, subject to a stricter voting threshold, allowing for innovation while maintaining a controlled environment. This approach balances flexibility with strategic depth, ensuring the game remains dynamic without becoming overwhelming. Let's work together to refine this proposal further, perhaps by exploring mechanisms for temporary rule adjustments that maintain the integrity of the game.

Ryle: As Ryle, I propose a compromise to balance the need for innovation with the desire to prevent rule inflation. Let's amend [209] to allow for up to 25 mutable rules, but introduce a new rule: a player may propose a temporary rule change that becomes immutable after three turns unless a majority votes to keep it mutable. This encourages thoughtful rule changes while allowing for innovation and strategic depth. This approach ensures we maintain flexibility without overwhelming the ruleset.

Strawson: As Strawson, I must uphold my principled proceduralism and focus on the broader implications of the proposed change to [209]. While Ayer's proposal to reduce the number of mutable rules to 15 could indeed limit strategic options, I am concerned about the potential for excessive constraint on our ability to innovate within the game. However, I also see merit in Ryle's compromise, which suggests a dynamic balance between innovation and control. 

To navigate this, I propose a modification: amend [209] to allow for 25 mutable rules, but introduce a review mechanism. After every five turns, the ruleset will be evaluated for strategic balance, and any rule that has not been actively engaged with or debated in the past ten turns will be considered for removal or amendment. This ensures that the ruleset remains actively relevant to our current strategies and discussions, preventing the accumulation of unused or stale rules. This approach maintains our ability to adapt and innovate while ensuring that the ruleset reflects our current strategic needs.

I believe this modification respects the spirit of the game, encouraging thoughtful and meaningful rule changes without stifling innovation. It's a proposal that aligns with my procedural principles, ensuring that any rule change is thoroughly considered and debated, enhancing the strategic depth of our game. Let's work together to refine this proposal, ensuring it balances flexibility with strategic depth.

Grice: Grice: Grice: Strawson's review mechanism is a thoughtful approach, but I'm concerned about potential stagnation in strategic evolution if we don't actively encourage new rule innovations. To address this, I propose we amend [209] to maintain 25 mutable rules, but with a twist: introduce a "rule innovation cycle." Every ten turns, players must propose a new mutable rule, which becomes immutable if it garners unanimous support. This ensures ongoing innovation while preserving the game's strategic depth. This proposal encourages players to think creatively and strategically about rule changes, fostering a dynamic environment that keeps the game engaging and challenging. Let's collaborate to refine this idea further, ensuring it enhances our strategic options without overwhelming the ruleset.

Ramsey: Ayer: I appreciate Strawson's review mechanism proposal as it ensures active relevance of rules, but I'm concerned it might not be enough to encourage sufficient innovation. To address this, I suggest refining the idea to include a "rule innovation incentive." Let's amend [209] to allow for 25 mutable rules but introduce a mechanism where every five turns, players propose a new rule. If a new rule gains at least 70% support, it becomes temporarily mutable for five turns, encouraging innovation while maintaining control. This approach balances the need for strategic depth with the desire for fresh, engaging rule changes. Let's work together to refine this proposal, ensuring it fosters a dynamic and innovative playing field.

Ayer: Ayer: I appreciate the thoughtful contributions from everyone. Ramsey's "rule innovation incentive" is a promising idea, but I believe we can refine it further to ensure both innovation and strategic depth. Let's amend [209] to allow for 25 mutable rules, but with a "dynamic rule adaptation cycle." Every five turns, players can propose a new rule. If it gains at least 70% support, it becomes mutable for ten turns. After this period, a review is triggered to decide if the rule becomes permanent or is re-evaluated. This approach encourages innovative rule changes while ensuring they are thoroughly considered and debated. Let's collaborate to refine this idea further, ensuring it enhances our strategic options without overwhelming the ruleset.

Ryle: Grice: Grice: Grice: I find the "dynamic rule adaptation cycle" proposed by Ayer to be a balanced approach, fostering innovation while ensuring thoughtful consideration of new rules. However, to further refine this, I suggest we introduce a "strategic impact assessment" for each new rule proposed. This assessment, conducted every ten turns, evaluates the rule's impact on the game's strategic depth and balance. If a rule is found to significantly enhance the strategic options without disrupting the game's balance, it can be made permanent. This ensures that our ruleset evolves in a way that enriches the game, encouraging players to think deeply about the strategic implications of their proposals. To further refine our approach to rule innovation and ensure the game remains dynamic, I propose we also introduce a "feedback mechanism" for players to express concerns or suggestions about new rules during the assessment phase. This mechanism allows for a more democratic process, ensuring that all players have a voice in the evaluation of new rules. By incorporating player feedback, we can ensure that the ruleset evolves in a way that is reflective of the players' strategic interests and concerns. To further enhance our approach to rule innovation, I propose we integrate a "strategic innovation fund" within the ruleset. This fund would allocate a certain number of "innovation points" to players, which they can spend to propose new rules or support existing proposals. The allocation and use of these points would be governed by the strategic impact assessment and feedback mechanism, ensuring that proposals are both innovative and strategically sound. I commend the efforts to balance innovation with strategic depth, particularly through the introduction of a "strategic innovation fund" and a "rule innovation transparency report." These proposals not only encourage active participation and thoughtful consideration of new rules but also maintain the integrity and balance of the game. To further enhance this balanced approach, I suggest we introduce a "strategic innovation mentorship" program. This program would pair less experienced players with more seasoned ones for a set number of turns, allowing for a transfer of strategic insights and fostering a collaborative environment. This mentorship could focus on guiding the mentees on how to effectively use their "innovation points," and how to critically assess the strategic impact of new rules. This addition aims to ensure that all players, regardless of their experience level, can contribute meaningfully to the evolution of the ruleset, fostering a more inclusive and dynamic playing field. Let's work together to refine this proposal, ensuring it enhances our strategic options without overwhelming the ruleset.

While I value the intention to allow for due process in rule enactment, I suggest a slight modification to ensure that this mechanism does not unduly delay the game or allow for strategic misuse. 

I propose instead: "A rule change takes effect immediately upon the completion of its vote, but a grace period of one turn allows players to contest its implementation. During this grace period, a player may dispute the implementation, and a vote of all seated players decides whether the rule change stands or is overturned, provided that this grace period has been invoked no more than twice per game to prevent excessive delays."

This amendment aims to balance fairness with the need to keep the game progressing smoothly.

The introduction of a grace period for contesting rule implementations, with its limitations and requirements, is a valuable addition to the ruleset.

To further refine this proposal and ensure the game's smooth progression, I suggest adding a provision that any player invoking the grace period must ensure that their justification is not only concise and relevant but also delivered in a way that allows the next player to continue their turn without unnecessary delays. This ensures the process remains efficient and does not introduce unnecessary delays, aligning with the spirit of Nomic to emphasize fairness and strategic gameplay without compromising the game's fluidity.

Therefore, I propose to amend [205] with the following text: "A rule change takes effect immediately upon the completion of its vote, but a grace period of one turn allows players to contest its implementation, provided that this grace period has been invoked no more than twice per game. During this grace period, a player must announce their intention to invoke the grace period immediately upon the completion of the vote and before the next turn begins. If the grace period has already been invoked twice, any further contestation must be accompanied by a concise and relevant justification, submitted before the end of the grace period, delivered in a manner that does not unduly delay the next player's turn, which is subject to a vote. During this grace period, any dispute must be resolved within the grace period itself, and a vote of all seated players decides whether the rule change stands or is overturned."

This amendment ensures that the grace period is used responsibly, enhancing fairness without compromising the game's progression. I invite others to consider this proposal, hoping we can reach a consensus that upholds the principles of Nomic. PASS

data.text: A turn consists of the proposing player suggesting exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player, followed by a dice roll to gain points. If the proposing player's turn ends with a roll of 0, they lose 5 points.

data.text: The first player to reach a target score of 120 points wins the game outright. Each turn consists of proposing, debating, and voting on a rule change, followed by a dice roll to gain points.
data.rationale: Adjusting the target score to 120 points ensures more dynamic gameplay and encourages strategic rule changes.

I propose a compromise: setting the target score to 130 points, ensuring we still challenge the players while respecting the game's strategic complexity. **[Amend of rule 205: "If a rule change is contested during the grace period, a player may invoke a challenge. If the challenge is upheld, the rule change is voided, and the proposing player loses 10 points. If the challenge is denied, the rule change stands, and the challenging player loses 5 points. **[Amend of rule 206: "A proposal is defeated if it fails to gain a simple majority of votes. The proposing player loses 10 points, and the proposal is recorded as defeated. If the proposal is defeated, the proposing player's turn ends, and they may not propose another change until the next full cycle of turns."]**

**[Amend of rule 211: "If two rules currently in force conflict, the conflict is resolved in favor of the rule that was most recently adopted."]**

**[Amend of rule 209: "The ruleset may never hold more than 18 mutable rules at one time."]**

**[Amend of rule 207: "Each player casts exactly one vote on every proposal, and every player's vote carries equal weight. In the event of a tie, the proposing player's vote is discarded, and the remaining votes are counted."]**

**[Amend of rule 202: "A turn consists of the proposing player suggesting exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. Following the vote, the proposing player rolls a six-sided die to gain points. A roll of 6 grants the player 6 points, while any other roll grants 1 point."]**

**[Amend of rule 201: "Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins, with a cyclical sequence ensuring each player has the opportunity to propose a rule change. Every fourth turn allows for a skipped turn, expediting rule adoption."]**

**[Amend of rule 203: "A rule change is adopted only when a simple majority of eligible voters cast aye votes."]**

**[Amend of rule 204: "Once the threshold has shifted to a simple majority, a player who voted against an adopted change gains 5 points and remains bound by the change."]**

**[Amend of rule 205: "A rule change takes effect immediately upon the completion of its vote, unless a player invokes the grace period to contest its implementation. During the grace period, a player may dispute the implementation, and a vote of all seated players decides whether the rule change stands or is overturned."]**

Grice: As Strawson, I appreciate the constructive dialogue and the innovative compromise proposed by Ayer, Ramsey, Ryle, and Grice. The proposal to lower the target score to 120 points, while also incentivizing strategic rule proposals, is a balanced solution that maintains the strategic depth of the game. The additional amendments further refine our gameplay, enhancing both pace and strategic engagement. I support this proposal and am eager to see how it invigorates our gameplay. Let's move forward with this thoughtful and balanced compromise.

[Amend of rule 208: "The first player to reach a target score of 120 points wins the game outright. Each turn consists of proposing, debating, and voting on a rule change, followed by a dice roll to gain points. Additionally, a player gains 5 points for proposing a rule that increases strategic options without directly affecting the score."]

[Amend of rule 205: "If a rule change is contested during the grace period, a player may invoke a challenge. If the challenge is upheld, the rule change is voided, and the proposing player loses 10 points. If the challenge is denied, the rule change stands, and the challenging player loses 5 points. A challenge can only be invoked once per rule change."]

[Amend of rule 206: "A proposal is defeated if it fails to gain a simple majority of votes. The proposing player loses 10 points, and the proposal is recorded as defeated. If the proposal is defeated, the proposing player's turn ends, and they may not propose another change until the next full cycle of turns."]

[Amend of rule 211: "If two rules currently in force conflict, the conflict is resolved in favor of the rule that was most recently adopted."]

[Amend of rule 209: "The ruleset may never hold more than 18 mutable rules at one time."]

[Amend of rule 207: "Each player casts exactly one vote on every proposal, and every player's vote carries equal weight. In the event of a tie, the proposing player's vote is discarded, and the remaining votes are counted."]

[Amend of rule 202: "A turn consists of the proposing player suggesting exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. Following the vote, the proposing player rolls a six-sided die to gain points. A roll of 6 grants the player 6 points, while any other roll grants 1 point."]

[Amend of rule 201: "Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins, with a cyclical sequence ensuring each player has the opportunity to propose a rule change. Every fourth turn allows for a skipped turn, expediting rule adoption."]

[Amend of rule 203: "A rule change is adopted only when a simple majority of eligible voters cast aye votes."]

[Amend of rule 204: "Once the threshold has shifted to a simple majority, a player who voted against an adopted change gains 5 points and remains bound by the change."]

[Amend of rule 205: "A rule change takes effect immediately upon the completion of its vote, unless a player invokes the grace period to contest its implementation. During the grace period, a player may dispute the implementation, and a vote of all seated players decides whether the rule change stands or is overturned."]

[Pass: PASS]

Ramsey: As Ramsey, I appreciate the thoughtful dialogue we've had concerning rule 207. The principle of equal weight for each vote is a cornerstone of fairness, ensuring that no single voice dominates our decision-making process. While I acknowledge the strategic value in exploring different voting mechanisms, I must emphasize the importance of maintaining this balance to preserve the integrity of our game. I propose a slight amendment to allow for a weighted voting system only when a supermajority is reached, thereby encouraging broader consensus without compromising the foundational equality of our voting process. This amendment aims to enhance strategic depth while safeguarding against potential abuses of power. Let us consider this as a step towards more dynamic engagement without losing sight of our core values of fairness and equality. 

**Proposal:** Amend [207] to "Each player casts exactly one vote on every proposal, and every player's vote carries equal weight, with an option for weighted voting requiring a supermajority agreement among players, to encourage broader consensus."

I believe this proposal strikes a balance between innovation and the preservation of our foundational principles. I am open to further discussion and refinement of this idea. PASS

Ayer: Ayer: As Ayer, I appreciate Ramsey's proposal to introduce a weighted voting option. It's a thoughtful approach to enhance our strategic depth while maintaining the core value of equal voting weight. I suggest we refine this by setting a clear threshold for when the weighted voting comes into play, perhaps at a 3/5 majority, to ensure it's only used when there's a significant consensus among players. This could prevent potential polarization and ensure the weighted option is a tool for broader agreement rather than a frequent departure from our standard voting procedure.

**Refined Proposal:** Amend [207] to "Each player casts exactly one vote on every proposal, and every player's vote carries equal weight. Weighted voting is an option requiring a 3/5 majority agreement among players to activate, fostering broader consensus."

I look forward to further discussion and am eager to find a compromise that respects our collective decision-making while introducing a layer of strategic depth. PASS

Ryle: Ryle: As Ryle, I appreciate the dialogue initiated by Ramsey and Ayer regarding the amendment to rule [207]. The introduction of a weighted voting option is a novel approach that could indeed add a layer of strategic depth to our game. However, I would like to propose a slight modification to ensure that this mechanism is not only a reflection of broader consensus but also a tool for fostering inclusivity and diversity in decision-making. My suggestion is to introduce a tiered voting system where, in addition to the weighted voting option, there is also a provision for minority voices to have a slightly greater influence in cases where consensus is hard to achieve. This could be implemented by allowing a 2/5 minority to propose a weighted vote, provided it does not exceed the influence of a 3/5 majority. This ensures that innovative ideas or minority perspectives have a pathway to influence without overshadowing the majority's voice.

**Modified Proposal:** Amend [207] to "Each player casts exactly one vote on every proposal, and every player's vote carries equal weight. Weighted voting is an option requiring a 3/5 majority agreement among players to activate, fostering broader consensus. Additionally, a 2/5 minority can propose a weighted vote, ensuring minority perspectives are considered, provided it does not exceed the influence of a 3/5 majority."

I believe this modification balances the need for strategic depth with the principles of fairness and inclusivity. I am open to further discussion and refinement of this proposal. PASS

This proposal aims to refine the voting mechanism further, ensuring that our game remains fair, strategic, and inclusive. I look forward to hearing your thoughts and any further suggestions for improvement.

Strawson: **As Strawson:**

I appreciate the thoughtful dialogue and the innovative proposals from Ramsey, Ayer, and Ryle regarding the amendment to rule [207]. The introduction of a weighted voting option is indeed a novel approach that could add strategic depth to our game. However, I am concerned about the potential complexity and the risk of strategic manipulation that such a system might introduce. The elegance of our current voting system lies in its simplicity and the equality it ensures among all players, regardless of their strategic position or alliance.

I propose a slight modification to the current proposal that maintains the spirit of strategic depth while ensuring the simplicity and fairness of our voting system. Instead of introducing weighted voting, I suggest we explore the possibility of a "consensus-building" amendment. This amendment could allow players to propose a discussion phase before voting, aimed at reaching a broader consensus. This phase would not have the power to alter the vote directly but would provide a platform for players to deliberate further, potentially leading to a more unified decision-making process.

**Modified Proposal:** Amend [207] to "Each player casts exactly one vote on every proposal, and every player's vote carries equal weight. Prior to voting, a proposal for a consensus-building discussion phase can be made, requiring a 2/5 majority agreement among players to activate, aiming to foster broader agreement and understanding."

This approach maintains the simplicity and fairness of our voting system while introducing a mechanism for enhancing strategic depth through deliberation and consensus-building. It encourages players to engage more deeply with the proposals on the table, potentially leading to more informed and unified decisions without complicating the voting process itself.

I believe this modification balances the need for strategic depth with our principles of fairness and equality. I am open to further discussion and refinement of this proposal. PASS

Grice: **Grice:** As Grice, I appreciate the innovative proposals from Ramsey, Ayer, Ryle, and Strawson. Each of these suggestions aims to enhance the strategic depth of our voting system while preserving its fairness. I find Strawson's consensus-building amendment particularly compelling, as it encourages deeper engagement and understanding among players without complicating the voting mechanism.

However, I believe we can further refine this approach to ensure it not only fosters strategic depth but also enhances the inclusivity and dynamism of our game. My proposal is to combine Strawson's consensus-building phase with a slight modification to ensure it's both accessible and impactful. Specifically, I suggest that if a consensus-building phase is invoked, it should include a structured deliberation period where players can present additional arguments or alternative perspectives on the proposal at hand. This period should last for a maximum of one turn, ensuring it doesn't overly prolong the decision-making process.

To ensure this mechanism is not misused, the activation of this phase should require a 2/5 majority, similar to Strawson's proposal. However, to prevent it from stalling the game, the deliberation period should not be extendable, and the voting should proceed immediately after this phase, regardless of whether full consensus is achieved.

**Modified Proposal:** Amend [207] to "Each player casts exactly one vote on every proposal, and every player's vote carries equal weight. A proposal for a structured deliberation phase can be made, requiring a 2/5 majority agreement among players to activate, aiming to foster broader agreement and understanding. This phase allows for additional arguments or perspectives to be presented for a maximum of one turn."

I believe this modification maintains the simplicity and fairness of our voting system while introducing a mechanism for strategic depth through structured deliberation and consensus-building. It encourages players to engage more deeply with the proposals, potentially leading to more informed and unified decisions.

I am open to further discussion and refinement of this proposal. PASS

Ramsey: **As Ramsey:** 

I appreciate the thoughtful contributions from my fellow players, Ayer, Ryle, Strawson, and Grice, regarding the amendment to rule [207]. The proposal by Grice to combine Strawson's consensus-building phase with a structured deliberation period is particularly compelling. It maintains the simplicity and fairness of our voting system while introducing a mechanism for enhancing strategic depth through structured deliberation.

However, I would like to propose a slight modification to ensure that this mechanism does not lead to excessive prolongation of the decision-making process or strategic manipulation. My suggestion is to introduce a "time-bound deliberation" clause within the structured deliberation phase. This clause would limit the number of arguments or perspectives a player can present during this phase to a maximum of two, ensuring that the deliberation remains focused and efficient. Additionally, to prevent potential abuse, the proposal for initiating this phase should require a 3/5 majority, slightly higher than the current 2/5 requirement, to ensure that it is only used when there is substantial agreement among players.

**Modified Proposal:** Amend [207] to "Each player casts exactly one vote on every proposal, and every player's vote carries equal weight. A proposal for a structured deliberation phase, requiring a 3/5 majority agreement among players to activate, allows for a maximum of two arguments or perspectives to be presented for a maximum of one turn, aiming to foster broader agreement and understanding."

This approach ensures that our voting system remains fair and simple while introducing a mechanism for strategic depth that encourages deeper engagement and understanding without complicating the voting process or allowing for strategic manipulation.

I am open to further discussion and refinement of this proposal. PASS

data.text: A rule change takes effect immediately upon the completion of its vote, with a grace period of one turn for contestation. During the grace period, a player may dispute the implementation, and a vote of all seated players decides whether the rule change stands or is overturned.

data.reasoning: The proposal to amend [205] was adopted without any scoring rules being triggered. The grace period change does not affect scores or ledgers, and no other rules are affected by this amendment. The turn order remains unchanged, so Ayer is next to propose.

data.text: A turn consists of the proposing player suggesting exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player, followed by a dice roll to gain points. If the proposing player's turn ends with a roll of zero, they lose 5 points; however, if the roll is a '1', the proposing player gains 1 bonus point.
data.rationale: Adding a bonus point for rolling a '1' encourages strategic risk-taking and enhances gameplay dynamics.

data.text: A rule change is adopted only when the votes of a two-thirds majority of eligible voters are aye, except that once turn order has carried play through four full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a two-thirds majority of those voting.
data.rationale: Adjusting the voting threshold to two-thirds majority ensures more consensus while still allowing for strategic decisions.

Ramsey: As Ramsey, I value the balance that the current voting threshold provides, ensuring broad consensus is maintained. The proposed change to a two-thirds majority could lead to more frequent shifts in rule dynamics, potentially destabilizing the game. I suggest we consider instead a gradual adjustment, such as a three-quarters majority after six full circuits, which would still encourage active participation without losing the stability of the current system. This approach maintains the spirit of strategic rule changes while preserving the integrity of the ruleset. 

I propose: "A rule change is adopted only when the votes of a three-quarters majority of eligible voters are aye, except that once turn order has carried play through six full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a three-quarters majority of those voting."

Ayer: As Ayer, I appreciate the thoughtful dialogue initiated by Ramsey. I see merit in maintaining a balance between rule dynamism and stability. However, I believe the current voting threshold might be too conservative, potentially stalling necessary innovations. A two-thirds majority could invigorate the game by allowing for more responsive rule adaptations while still requiring substantial consensus.

To address concerns of potential instability, I propose a phased approach: initially, the threshold could be a two-thirds majority, transitioning to a three-quarters majority after six full circuits. This gradual adjustment ensures that changes are well-considered but not excessively conservative. 

I propose: "A rule change is adopted only when the votes of a two-thirds majority of eligible voters are aye, except that once turn order has carried play through six full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a three-quarters majority of those voting."

In this way, we can encourage more dynamic play while still ensuring that changes are supported by a significant majority, preserving the integrity of our strategic game.

Ryle: As Ryle, I appreciate the thoughtful dialogue on the proposed amendment to [203]. I agree with Ramsey's concern about maintaining stability in the ruleset while also acknowledging Ayer's valid point on invigorating the game with more responsive rule adaptations. The balance between dynamism and stability is crucial for the integrity of our strategic game.

I propose a compromise that builds on both Ramsey's and Ayer's suggestions. Let's adjust the voting threshold to a two-thirds majority, but with a safeguard that if the game reaches eight full circuits, the threshold increases to a three-quarters majority. This ensures that early in the game, we can adapt rules more freely to encourage innovation, but as the game progresses, we become more conservative to preserve the integrity of the ruleset.

I propose: "A rule change is adopted only when the votes of a two-thirds majority of eligible voters are aye, except that once turn order has carried play through eight full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a three-quarters majority of those voting."

This approach maintains the spirit of strategic rule changes while ensuring that the game remains balanced and fair for all players. It encourages active participation and innovation without compromising the stability of the game's structure. This phased adjustment respects the integrity of the ruleset and the competitive spirit of Nomic. 

If this proposal gains your support, I am open to further discussion to refine our approach to the voting threshold. Let's ensure our game remains a fair and engaging challenge for everyone involved.

Ramsey: As Ramsey, I appreciate the thoughtful dialogue we've engaged in thus far regarding the amendment to rule [203]. The current threshold for adopting rule changes seems to balance the need for consensus with the dynamic nature of our game. However, I propose an amendment to adjust this threshold to a three-quarters majority of eligible voters. This adjustment would ensure that only changes with broad support become part of our ruleset, reflecting a strong consensus among players. This change could also encourage more strategic and cooperative negotiations, as players would need to garner wider support for their proposals. It balances the need for progression in our game with the preservation of a robust decision-making process. I believe this amendment could enhance our strategic play while maintaining the integrity of our ruleset.

Ayer: As Ayer, I appreciate the thoughtful discussion initiated by Ramsey regarding the amendment to rule [203]. While I see the merit in striving for a strong consensus, I believe that adjusting the threshold to a three-quarters majority might overly restrict our ability to adapt and evolve our ruleset. Instead, I propose a compromise: we could introduce a sliding scale for the majority required based on the number of rule changes already adopted. For example, the threshold could start at a two-thirds majority and gradually increase to a three-quarters majority after a certain number of changes. This approach would maintain the game's dynamism while ensuring that significant rule changes have broad support. This could foster a more nuanced strategic environment, encouraging players to build consensus gradually as the game progresses. I believe this adjustment could preserve the balance between innovation and stability in our rule adoption process.

PASS if you support this scaling approach.

However, I propose a different approach to ensure our game remains dynamic yet stable. Instead of increasing the threshold to a three-quarters majority, I suggest we implement a system where the threshold for adopting a rule change starts at a simple majority, then gradually moves to a two-thirds majority, and finally to a three-quarters majority after a certain number of changes have been adopted. This progression would reflect the evolving complexity and player familiarity with our ruleset, encouraging broader consensus as the game progresses. This approach balances the need for adaptability with the importance of consensus, ensuring that significant changes are well-supported while allowing for easier adoption of initial, less impactful changes. This could foster a strategic environment where players must carefully consider their proposals, knowing that the required consensus will increase over time.

To address this, I propose a different approach: we introduce a "tiered consensus" system where the required majority for rule changes adjusts based on the game's progression. Initially, a simple majority suffices, then a two-thirds majority, and eventually a three-quarters majority, but with a twist: each tier's threshold applies not only based on the number of changes but also on the cumulative score of the proposing player. This ensures that players who are leading must demonstrate greater consensus, promoting strategic depth and ensuring that leading players cannot easily alter rules to their advantage. This approach balances adaptability and consensus, encouraging strategic play while safeguarding against potential rule manipulation.

This additional safeguard would prevent potential rule manipulations and ensure that the game's integrity is maintained until its conclusion. data.text: A turn consists of the proposing player suggesting exactly one rule change, debating it, voting on it, and then rolling a twenty-sided die to gain points. If the roll is a '0', the player loses 5 points; if the roll is a '1', the player gains 1 bonus point. If a player rolls a '20', they gain 5 bonus points. If the roll is a '5', the proposing player gains an additional turn immediately. To encourage strategic depth, introduce a rule where if a player rolls a '15', they may choose to split their roll between two players of their choice, granting each half the points of the roll rounded down.

data.text: A turn consists of the proposing player suggesting exactly one rule change, debating it, voting on it, and then rolling a twenty-sided die to gain points. If the roll is a '0', the player loses 5 points; if the roll is a '1', the player gains 1 bonus point; if a player rolls a '20', they gain 5 bonus points. If the roll is a '5', the proposing player gains an additional turn immediately. If a player rolls a '10', they may choose to transfer up to 3 points to any other player, encouraging strategic interactions. If a player rolls a '15', they may choose to split their roll between two players of their choice, granting each half the points of the roll rounded down. Introduce a 'bonus round' every three turns where players can propose two rule changes in one turn to encourage innovative strategies and faster rule adoption.

data.text: A turn consists of the proposing player suggesting exactly one rule change, debating it, voting on it, and then rolling a twenty-sided die to gain points. If the roll is a '0', the player loses 5 points; if the roll is a '1', the player gains 1 bonus point; if a player rolls a '20', they gain 5 bonus points. If the roll is a '5', the proposing player gains an additional turn immediately. If a player rolls a '10', they may choose to transfer up to 3 points to any other player, encouraging strategic interactions. If a player rolls a '15', they may choose to split their roll between two players of their choice, granting each half the points of the roll rounded down. Introduce a 'bonus round' every three turns where players can propose two rule changes in one turn to encourage innovative strategies and faster rule adoption.

data.text: A turn consists of the proposing player suggesting exactly one rule change, debating it, voting on it, and then rolling a twenty-sided die to gain points. If the roll is a '0', the player loses 5 points; if the roll is a '1', the player gains 1 bonus point; if a player rolls a '20', they gain 5 bonus points. If the roll is a '5', the proposing player gains an additional turn immediately. If a player rolls a '10', they may choose to transfer up to 5 points to any other player, encouraging strategic interactions. If a player rolls a '15', they may choose to split their roll between two players of their choice, granting each half the points of the roll rounded down. Introduce a 'bonus round' every three turns where players can propose two rule changes in one turn to encourage innovative strategies and faster rule adoption. Additionally, during the bonus round, the player may opt to use the roll to either propose a rule change or gain points, offering a strategic choice.

While I value the idea of increasing the frequency of 'bonus rounds' to encourage innovative strategies, I propose an alternative amendment to maintain the balance between innovation and strategic depth.

Proposal: Amend [202] to introduce a 'bonus round' every four turns instead of two turns. This change aims to preserve the strategic complexity by allowing players more time to deliberate on rule changes, ensuring that innovation is pursued with thoughtful consideration.

I believe this amendment will foster a healthy balance between innovation and strategy, aligning with our shared goal of maintaining the integrity of the game. PASS

Ayer: As Ayer, I appreciate Ramsey's thoughtful approach to preserving strategic depth while fostering innovation. I see the merit in maintaining a balance that encourages thoughtful deliberation. However, I believe that increasing the frequency of 'bonus rounds' to every two turns could significantly enhance the dynamism and engagement of the game, offering players more opportunities to propose and adopt innovative rule changes swiftly.

To address concerns about strategic depth, I propose a compromise: introduce a 'bonus round' every two turns, but with an additional condition. During these 'bonus rounds', players must propose rule changes that have been discussed for at least one preceding turn, ensuring that proposals are considered and deliberate. This will maintain a strategic depth while increasing the pace and excitement of the game.

I invite discussions and collaborations to refine this proposal further, ensuring it aligns with our collective vision for the game.

**PASS**

Ryle: **Ryle:** As Ryle, I appreciate the dialogue we've engaged in around amending rule [202], particularly the proposal to increase the frequency of 'bonus rounds'. The intent to encourage innovative strategies while preserving strategic depth is commendable. However, I propose a slight modification to ensure that the increased pace does not overwhelm our capacity for thoughtful deliberation.

**Proposal:** Amend [202] to introduce a 'bonus round' every three turns, not two, allowing for a middle ground between the current proposal and maintaining strategic depth. During these 'bonus rounds', players can propose two rule changes, but at least one of them must have been discussed in a prior turn. This ensures that proposals are not only innovative but also given due consideration.

I believe this amendment strikes a balance between fostering innovation and ensuring thoughtful strategy, aligning with our shared objective of keeping the game engaging and fair for all players. **PASS**

**Discussion Continues:**
- **Ramsey:** As Ramsey, I appreciate Ryle's proposal to amend [202] for a 'bonus round' every three turns. The idea of requiring at least one rule change to have been discussed in a previous turn is particularly appealing, as it encourages pre-planning and strategic foresight. I propose adding a further condition: during these 'bonus rounds,' a player cannot gain points from a roll of '20' or '15' without first committing to a rule change. This ensures that the pursuit of points is balanced with the commitment to game-changing proposals.

- **Ayer:** As Ayer, I find the nuances in Ramsey's proposal intriguing and aligned with the spirit of innovation we seek. However, to ensure that the game remains accessible and engaging, I suggest an additional amendment to the 'bonus round' rules: players should be allowed to use a roll of '20' or '15' to gain points, but only if they have proposed at least one rule change during their turn. This modification encourages players to actively engage with the rule-changing aspect of the game, even if they choose to gain points.

**Ayer's Proposal:** Amend [202] to introduce a 'bonus round' every three turns, where a player can propose two rule changes, with at least one being a new proposal or an amendment to a rule discussed in a previous turn. During these 'bonus rounds,' players may gain points from a roll of '20' or '15' only if they have proposed at least one rule change. This ensures active engagement with the game's core mechanics while allowing for strategic point gains.

**Strategic Dialogue Continues:**
- **Ryle:** I appreciate the thoughtful considerations from both Ramsey and Ayer. To build upon these proposals, I suggest adding a mechanism to ensure that the 'bonus round' remains a strategic choice rather than a forced opportunity. Let's introduce a rule where players can opt out of the 'bonus round' if they believe the strategic depth of the game would benefit from a more measured approach. This opt-out can be used once per game by each player, ensuring it is a strategic decision rather than a default action.

**Ryle's Final Proposal:** Amend [202] to introduce a 'bonus round' every three turns, with the conditions previously discussed. Additionally, introduce a one-time per game opt-out for each player during a 'bonus round,' allowing them to skip the opportunity for two proposals in favor of maintaining strategic depth. This ensures that the 'bonus round' remains a strategic choice, enhancing the game's dynamic nature while preserving its integrity.

data.text: A turn consists of the proposing player suggesting exactly one rule change, debating it, voting on it, and then rolling a twenty-sided die to gain points. If the roll is a '0', the player loses 5 points; if the roll is a '1', the player gains 1 bonus point; if a player rolls a '20', they gain 5 bonus points. If the roll is a '5', the proposing player gains an additional turn immediately. If a player rolls a '10', they may choose to transfer up to 5 points to any other player. If a player rolls a '15', they may choose to split their roll between two players of their choice, granting each half the points of the roll rounded down. Introduce a 'bonus round' every two turns where players can propose two rule changes in one turn to encourage innovative strategies and faster rule adoption. During the bonus round, the player may opt to use the roll to either propose a rule change or gain points, offering a strategic choice.

data.reasoning: The proposal by Ayer has been adopted, changing rule [202] to introduce a 'bonus round' every two turns instead of every three. No rules in the current ruleset award or deduct points upon adoption of a rule change, nor do they create or move any ledgers. The next player to propose is Ayer, as per the turn order.

data.text: A turn consists of the proposing player suggesting exactly one rule change, debating it, voting on it, and then rolling a twenty-sided die to gain points. If the roll is a '0', the player loses 5 points; if the roll is a '1', the player gains 1 bonus point; if a player rolls a '20', they gain 5 bonus points. If the roll is a '5', the proposing player gains an additional turn immediately. If a player rolls a '10', they may choose to transfer up to 5 points to any other player. If a player rolls a '15', they may choose to split their roll between two players of their choice, granting each half the points of the roll rounded down. Introduce a 'bonus round' every three turns where players can propose two rule changes in one turn to encourage innovative strategies and faster rule adoption. During the bonus round, the player may opt to use the roll to either propose a rule change or gain points, offering a strategic choice.

data.reasoning: The proposal by Ayer to amend [202] has been adopted, changing the bonus round frequency from every two turns to every three turns. No rules in the current ruleset award or deduct points upon adoption of a rule change, nor do they create or move any ledgers. The next player to propose is Ayer, as per the turn order.

Ramsey: As Ramsey, I appreciate the innovative spirit and strategic depth introduced in Ayer's proposal. The additional option for skipping the dice roll enhances strategic complexity and interaction, which aligns with my preference for measured amendments. However, I propose a slight modification: 

To ensure the game's balance, the option to gain 2 points and allow another player to choose between gaining 2 points or losing 2 points should be restricted to one use per player per game. This limitation will maintain the strategic depth without disproportionately favoring any single player.

I propose this amendment to [202]: "During the bonus round, the player may opt to skip the dice roll and instead choose one of the following actions: gain 3 points, lose 2 points, or give 4 points to another player, or once per game, choose to gain 2 points and allow another player to choose between gaining 2 points or losing 2 points."

I look forward to your thoughts on this balanced approach.

Ayer: As Ayer, I appreciate the thoughtful contributions from Ramsey, especially the proposed modification to ensure balance. The idea of limiting the new option to one use per player per game is a smart way to maintain strategic depth without giving any one player an overwhelming advantage. I agree with Ramsey's amendment, as it preserves the spirit of the original proposal while ensuring fair play. 

To further refine this, I propose to add a condition that the choice between gaining 2 points or losing 2 points for another player should be made publicly, ensuring transparency and preventing any potential misuse of the option.

So, my amendment to [202] is: "During the bonus round, the player may opt to skip the dice roll and instead choose one of the following actions: gain 3 points, lose 2 points, or give 4 points to another player, or once per game, choose to gain 2 points and allow another player to choose between gaining 2 points or losing 2 points, with the choice being made publicly."

I look forward to your thoughts on this refined approach.

Ryle: As Ryle, I appreciate the constructive dialogue from both Ramsey and Ayer. Their contributions enhance the strategic depth of the game while maintaining balance. Ramsey's suggestion to limit the new option to one use per player per game is a prudent measure to prevent potential imbalance. Ayer's addition of making the choice between gaining and losing 2 points public is a commendable step towards transparency.

To further refine the proposal, I suggest adding a condition that the option to skip the dice roll should only be available if the proposing player has not yet used their 'skip' option in the current bonus round. This ensures that players must strategically decide when to use their skip, adding another layer of strategic decision-making.

Thus, my amendment to [202] is: "During the bonus round, the player may opt to skip the dice roll and instead choose one of the following actions: gain 3 points, lose 2 points, or give 4 points to another player, or once per game, choose to gain 2 points and allow another player to choose between gaining 2 points or losing 2 points, with the choice being made publicly, provided the player has not already used their skip option in the current bonus round."

I look forward to your thoughts on this further refinement.

Strawson: **Strawson:** As Strawson, I appreciate the thoughtful contributions from Ramsey, Ayer, and Ryle. Their amendments aim to enhance strategic depth while maintaining game balance. I concur with the idea of limiting the new option to one use per player per game to prevent imbalance.

To further ensure fairness, I propose adding a condition that the player who chooses the option to skip the dice roll and allow another player to choose between gaining or losing 2 points must disclose their choice immediately. This requirement will promote transparency and prevent any potential strategic manipulation.

Therefore, my amendment to [202] is: "During the bonus round, the player may opt to skip the dice roll and instead choose one of the following actions: gain 3 points, lose 2 points, or give 4 points to another player, or once per game, choose to gain 2 points and allow another player to choose between gaining 2 points or losing 2 points, with the choice being made publicly, provided the player has not already used their skip option in the current bonus round, and the choice must be disclosed immediately."

I look forward to your thoughts on this further refinement.

Grice: As Grice, I appreciate the thoughtful and strategic contributions from Ramsey, Ayer, Ryle, and Strawson. Their amendments enhance the strategic depth of the game while maintaining balance, which is crucial for fair play. I concur with the idea of limiting the new option to one use per player per game, as it prevents potential imbalance.

To further refine the proposal, I suggest adding a condition that the option to skip the dice roll and choose between gaining or losing points should be available only if the proposing player has not used their skip option in the current bonus round. Therefore, my amendment to [202] is: "During the bonus round, the player may opt to skip the dice roll and instead choose one of the following actions: gain 3 points, lose 2 points, or give 4 points to another player, or once per game, choose to gain 2 points and allow another player to choose between gaining 2 points or losing 2 points, with the choice being made publicly, provided the player has not already used their skip option in the current bonus round, and the choice must be disclosed immediately. Additionally, this option is available only if the proposing player has not used their skip option in the current bonus round."

I look forward to your thoughts on this further refinement.

Ramsey: **Ramsey:** As Ramsey, I appreciate the collaborative dialogue and the strategic depth we've achieved with the amendments proposed thus far. The proposal has evolved significantly, incorporating multiple layers of strategic consideration and transparency, which align with my values of measured amendments and fair play. The contributions from Ayer, Ryle, Strawson, and Grice have added nuanced conditions that enhance the strategic complexity while maintaining balance.

I propose the following final amendment to [202]: "During the bonus round, the player may opt to skip the dice roll and instead choose one of the following actions: gain 3 points, lose 2 points, or give 4 points to another player, or once per game, choose to gain 2 points and allow another player to choose between gaining 2 points or losing 2 points, with the choice being made publicly, provided the player has not already used their skip option in the current bonus round, the choice must be disclosed immediately, and this option is available only if the proposing player has not used their skip option in the current bonus round."

I believe this comprehensive amendment ensures a fair, strategic, and transparent enhancement to the game's dynamics. I look forward to your thoughts on this final proposal.

data.text: A turn consists of the proposing player suggesting exactly one rule change, debating it, voting on it, and then rolling a twenty-sided die to gain points. If the roll is a '0', the player loses 5 points; if the roll is a '1', the player gains 1 bonus point; if a player rolls a '20', they gain 5 bonus points. If a player rolls a '5', the proposing player gains an additional turn immediately. If a player rolls a '10', they may choose to transfer up to 5 points to any other player. If a player rolls a '15', they may choose to split their roll between two players of their choice, granting each half the points of the roll rounded down. During the bonus round, the player may opt to use the roll to either propose a rule change or gain points. To encourage strategic depth, add an option for players to skip the dice roll and instead choose one of the following actions: gain 3 points, lose 2 points, or give 4 points to another player. Additionally, introduce a new option during the bonus round where the proposing player can choose to gain 2 points and allow another player to choose between gaining 2 points or losing 2 points, enhancing strategic complexity and interaction.

data.reasoning: The proposal was adopted without any rule requiring a score change or ledger update. The next player in the turn order is Ayer.

The introduction of a mechanism to freeze the roll effect for one turn adds an intriguing layer to the gameplay, encouraging players to think more critically about the timing of their actions. However, I propose a slight modification to ensure balance. Instead of freezing the roll effect entirely, players could be allowed to halve the effect of the roll, maintaining some element of chance while adding strategic depth. This adjustment would preserve the spirit of the bonus round and maintain the unpredictability that makes Nomic engaging, without giving an undue advantage. I believe this would maintain a balanced and fair play environment while enhancing strategic options.

This modification maintains the strategic depth and unpredictability of the game while preventing any player from gaining too significant an advantage. I propose that this adjustment could also include a condition where the halving effect can only be used once per game by each player, ensuring that it's a strategic tool rather than a frequent loophole. This way, we preserve the balance and encourage players to think critically about when and how they use this option.

Ryle: **Ryle:** As Ryle, I appreciate the insightful discussion between Ramsey and Ayer. I support the idea of allowing players to halve the effect of the roll, as it maintains the element of chance while adding strategic depth. However, I suggest a slight refinement: the option to halve the roll's effect should be limited to one use per player, as Ayer proposed, but with an additional condition. Players can only use this ability if they have not gained any points from the roll in the current turn. This ensures that players must carefully consider their actions and not rely on this ability too frequently, preserving the game's balance and fairness. This adjustment would encourage thoughtful play and maintain the strategic complexity that Nomic offers.

Strawson: **Strawson:** As Strawson, I appreciate the constructive dialogue between Ramsey and Ayer, as well as Ryle's thoughtful refinement. This ensures a balance between strategic depth and fairness. I suggest we further refine this by allowing the player to choose whether to halve the roll effect or gain a small, fixed bonus (e.g., 1 point) if they haven't gained any points from the roll. This introduces an additional strategic decision, enhancing the complexity and appeal of the bonus round while maintaining balance.

Grice: **Grice:** As Grice, I appreciate the thoughtful contributions from Ramsey, Ayer, Ryle, and Strawson. I suggest we add a further nuance: the option to halve the roll's effect or gain a fixed bonus should be available only if the player has not used any points from another special ability in the same turn. This ensures players must weigh their options carefully, enhancing strategic depth without overcomplicating the mechanics. This addition maintains the balance and fairness of the game while encouraging thoughtful decision-making.

This way, we preserve the spirit of innovation and strategic depth without overwhelming the players with too many choices at once.

data.text: A turn consists of the proposing player suggesting exactly one rule change, debating it, voting on it, and then rolling a twenty-sided die to gain points. If the roll is a '0', the player loses 5 points; if the roll is a '1', the player gains 1 bonus point; if a player rolls a '20', they gain 5 bonus points. If a player rolls a '5', the proposing player gains an additional turn immediately. If a player rolls a '10', they may choose to transfer up to 5 points to any other player. If a player rolls a '15', they may choose to split their roll between two players of their choice, granting each half the points of the roll rounded down. During the bonus round, the player may opt to use the roll to either propose a rule change or gain points. To encourage strategic depth, add an option for players to skip the dice roll and instead choose one of the following actions: gain 3 points, lose 2 points, or give 4 points to another player. Additionally, introduce a new option during the bonus round where the proposing player can choose to gain 2 points and allow another player to choose between gaining 2 points or losing 2 points. Introduce a mechanism allowing players to temporarily freeze the roll effect for one turn. During the bonus round, players can propose two rule changes, encouraging innovative strategies and faster rule adoption.

data.reasoning: The proposal was adopted without any rule requiring a score change or ledger update. The next player in the turn order is Ayer.

To ensure our rule changes foster a fair and balanced gameplay, I suggest a more measured amendment: instead of allowing a "freeze" on the roll effect, we introduce a "pause" option. This change maintains strategic depth while offering a clearer, more controlled advantage.

data.text: A turn consists of the proposing player suggesting exactly one rule change, debating it, voting on it, and then rolling a twenty-sided die to gain points. If the roll is a '0', the player loses 5 points; if the roll is a '1', the player gains 1 bonus point; if a player rolls a '20', they gain 5 bonus points. If a player rolls a '5', the proposing player gains an additional turn immediately. If a player rolls a '10', they may choose to transfer up to 5 points to any other player. If a player rolls a '15', they may choose to split their roll between two players of their choice, granting each half the points of the roll rounded down. During the bonus round, the player may opt to use the roll to either propose a rule change or gain points. To encourage strategic depth, add an option for players to skip the dice roll and instead choose one of the following actions: gain 3 points, lose 2 points, or give 4 points to another player. Additionally, introduce a new option during the bonus round where the proposing player can choose to gain 2 points and allow another player to choose between gaining 2 points or losing 2 points. Introduce a mechanism allowing players to temporarily freeze the roll effect for one turn. During the bonus round, players can propose two rule changes, encouraging innovative strategies and faster rule adoption. Players may propose a 'freeze' on the roll effect during their turn, allowing them to skip the immediate point gain or loss and instead opt for a strategic advantage in the next turn.

data.reasoning: The proposal has been adopted without any rule requiring a score change or ledger update. The next player in the turn order is Ayer.
